results.title: How to Implement Webhook Idempotency
results.snippet: Automatic retries are the primary source of duplicate webhook deliveries. When a delivery attempt fails — due to a timeout, server error, or network issue — the provider (or your webhook infrastructure) retries the same event.
snippets: Automatic retries are the primary source of duplicate webhook deliveries. When a delivery attempt fails — due to a timeout, server error, or network issue — the provider (or your webhook infrastructure) retries the same event.

Automatic retries are the primary source of duplicate webhook deliveries. When a delivery attempt fails — due to a timeout, server error, or network issue — the provider (or your webhook infrastructure) retries the same event. Without idempotent handlers, every retry triggers your business logic again.

Otherwise, late retries will slip through. Event replay — deliberately re-delivering events for debugging or recovery — is one of the most powerful tools in webhook infrastructure. After an outage, a bug fix, or a configuration change, you can replay all failed events to recover without data loss. Replay is only safe when your handlers are idempotent. Without idempotency, replaying events that were partially processed could cause duplicate side effects.

It automatically suppresses duplicate deliveries before they reach your handlers using content-based deduplication, groups related failures into Issues so you can see root causes and bulk-retry affected events with one click, and retries failed deliveries with configurable backoff so transient errors never produce duplicate side effects. Every delivery is traced end-to-end with full payload, headers, and response data. Get started with Hookdeck to add production-grade reliability to your webhook pipeline without writing the queueing code yourself. For more on how delivery guarantees interact with idempotency, see Webhook Delivery Guarantees.

For a comprehensive approach to webhook reliability including retries, replay, and observability, see Taking Control of Your Webhook Reliability. ... Webhook idempotency means that processing the same webhook event multiple times produces the same result as processing it once. This is essential because webhook providers use at-least-once delivery, meaning duplicate events are expected.

results.title: Webhook Reliability 2026: Idempotency & Retry Reference
results.snippet: Frame it as a delivery guarantee and you are chasing a theorem that says no. The single most useful artifact for a multi-vendor webhook integration is a matrix that puts retry policy, signature scheme, and deduplication semantics side by side.
snippets: Frame it as a delivery guarantee and you are chasing a theorem that says no. The single most useful artifact for a multi-vendor webhook integration is a matrix that puts retry policy, signature scheme, and deduplication semantics side by side.

When a product manager asks for "guaranteed delivery," the correct answer is that delivery cannot be guaranteed exactly once — but processing can be made exactly-once through idempotency plus reconciliation. Frame the requirement as a processing guarantee and the engineering becomes tractable. Frame it as a delivery guarantee and you are chasing a theorem that says no. The single most useful artifact for a multi-vendor webhook integration is a matrix that puts retry policy, signature scheme, and deduplication semantics side by side.

Treating delivery as exactly-once is a category error; build for duplicates by default. ... Idempotency is the consumer's job, keyed on a stable event ID.Every well-designed webhook carries a unique event ID that stays constant across retries — Stripe's event id, Shopify's X-Shopify-Webhook-Id, Svix's webhook-id.

The implication is that duplicate handling is your responsibility, not the provider's. You make processing idempotent so that receiving an event a second time produces no second side effect, rather than relying on the network to never duplicate. Why is exactly-once delivery impossible, and what can I do instead? How do I implement idempotency in a webhook consumer? What's the difference between Stripe, Shopify, and Svix retry behavior?

The practical consequence is a clean distinction worth carrying into every design review: exactly-once is achievable as a processing guarantee, never as a delivery guarantee. The wire will sometimes deliver a duplicate. What you control is whether processing that duplicate produces a second side effect. That is the entire job of idempotency, and it sits on the consumer side — the receiver — not the sender.

results.title: Webhook Retries and Idempotency: A Practical Guide
results.snippet: This is the most common source of duplicate processing. An operation is idempotent if applying it multiple times produces the same result as applying it once. In the context of webhook handlers, this means: processing the same event twice must not create duplicate side effects.
snippets: This is the most common source of duplicate processing. An operation is idempotent if applying it multiple times produces the same result as applying it once. In the context of webhook handlers, this means: processing the same event twice must not create duplicate side effects.

If your handler is not idempotent, that results in duplicate orders, duplicate charges, or duplicate emails. This guide explains how retries work and how to build handlers that are safe to call multiple times. ... A webhook delivery is an HTTP request from someone else's server to yours.

The sender sees a timeout, marks delivery as failed, and retries — even though your handler may have completed the work. This is the most common source of duplicate processing. An operation is idempotent if applying it multiple times produces the same result as applying it once. In the context of webhook handlers, this means: processing the same event twice must not create duplicate side effects.

Deduplication at the handler entry point handles exact retries (same delivery ID). You also need to design the operations inside your handler to be safe when run multiple times — because not all duplicate deliveries carry the same delivery ID. When creating or updating records, use upsert semantics — insert if not exists, update if exists.

Your dedup logic must also handle business-level deduplication — checking whether the underlying entity (payment, order) has already been processed, not just the delivery. Calling Stripe, sending emails, or writing to S3 inside the webhook handler adds latency and failure modes. Any of these can cause a timeout, triggering retries. Enqueue the event and do external calls in a background worker. If your handler creates an order but crashes before sending the confirmation email, the retry will attempt both steps again. Design each step to be independently idempotent, not just the handler entry point.

results.snippet: With retries handled through idempotency and ordering handled through state checks, webhook delivery becomes predictable rather than dangerous. Retries introduce duplicate deliveries. Duplicate deliveries require idempotent processing.

Webhook security complements idempotency and state‑based processing by ensuring that only trusted events reach business logic. The table below shows simple, real‑world situations where reliable webhook handling is important. ... This is especially important for systems that manage legal documents, compliance data, and user trust. Webhooks use at least‑once delivery, which makes retries and duplicates inevitable.

From the application’s perspective, a retry is indistinguishable from a duplicate event. This leads to an important conclusion. Retries are not something to eliminate. They are something to handle safely. Handling retries safely requires webhook processing to behave the same way whether an event is received once or multiple times. This is where idempotency becomes essential.

Idempotency means that processing the same webhook multiple times results in the same final outcome as processing it once. In simple terms, if the same event arrives again, nothing bad should happen. ... Without idempotency, even a single retry can create user‑visible failures. However, idempotency alone does not solve every problem. Even when duplicates are handled safely, systems can still fail if events arrive in the wrong order.

results.snippet: If the same request arrives multiple times (provider retry, user double-click, clock skew), your system returns the first successful result and ignores duplicates. Core idea: Detect duplicate attempts and short-circuit processing.

Idempotent Webhook Retries in n8n (Without Duplicates) A practical guide to designing bulletproof retries in n8n using idempotency keys, safe persistence, and exactly-once semantics. Learn how to …

Webhooks fail. Networks blink. Providers retry. Your workflow dutifully runs… twice. Or three times. You ship duplicate invoices and apologize in Slack. Let’s be real — retries without idempotency is chaos.

Idempotency keys are unique request identifiers that guarantee at-most-once processing for a given operation. If the same request arrives multiple times (provider retry, user double-click, clock skew), your system returns the first successful result and ignores duplicates. Core idea: Detect duplicate attempts and short-circuit processing. Provider --POST--> n8n Webhook ---> Guard (idempotency check) | yes (seen) v Return cached result ^ | no (new) Run workflow --> Store outcome

A Webhook trigger that extracts an Idempotency-Key (or computes one). A Guard step that checks a persistence store (Redis/Postgres) for that key. A Process path that runs the workflow exactly once. A Persist step that stores the outcome for future duplicates. A Retry policy (exponential backoff + eventual consistency) that is safe.

raw_query.original: webhook idempotency best practices duplicate delivery retry semantics

evidence: Consumer-side idempotency is required: Webhook providers use at-least-once delivery, making duplicates inevitable. The consumer must deduplicate using a stable event ID.
Guard before side effects: An idempotency check (guard) must execute before any business logic or side effects (e.g., ledger reservations, notifications) to short-circuit duplicate deliveries.

Failure and retry semantics: If processing fails (e.g., exception during side effects), the event ID must not be recorded as successfully processed, ensuring the provider's retry will re-attempt processing.

Persistence vs In-Memory: While in-memory sets can track processed events, production best practices recommend persistent stores (Redis, Postgres) to survive process restarts and prevent duplicate processing after crashes.

Background processing: To avoid timeouts that trigger provider retries, heavy side effects should be enqueued to background workers, and the webhook handler should quickly return a 200 OK after persisting the event.
content.notes: External guidance strongly aligns with the need to move the `processed_events` check to the very beginning of the handler. It also highlights that relying on an in-memory set is risky for production due to restarts, and side-effects should ideally be enqueued or made independently idempotent.

"""Webhook event processing for fulfillment."""

from __future__ import annotations

from typing import Any

from .inventory import InventoryLedger
from .notifications import NotificationSink


def process_webhook_event(
    event: dict[str, Any],
    *,
    ledger: InventoryLedger,
    notifier: NotificationSink,
    processed_events: set[str],
) -> str:
    """Process a payment-provider webhook event.

    BUG: duplicate event IDs are not checked before side effects, so provider
    redelivery can reserve inventory and notify fulfillment more than once.
    """

    event_id = str(event["event_id"])
    event_type = str(event["type"])
    if event_type != "charge.succeeded":
        return "ignored"

    payload = event["data"]
    sku = str(payload["sku"])
    quantity = int(payload["quantity"])
    order_id = str(payload["order_id"])

    ledger.reserve(sku=sku, quantity=quantity, event_id=event_id)
    notifier.send_reserved(order_id=order_id, event_id=event_id, sku=sku, quantity=quantity)
    processed_events.add(event_id)
    return "reserved"

content.notes: Added an idempotency guard `if event_id in processed_events: return 'duplicate_ignored'` immediately after the `event_type` check and before any side effects. This prevents duplicate inventory reservations and notifications for repeated successful event deliveries, while allowing retries for events that failed before being added to `processed_events`.
evidence: Target file: src/fulfillment/events.py
Insertion point: After `if event_type != 'charge.succeeded': return 'ignored'`
Guard logic: `if event_id in processed_events: return 'duplicate_ignored'`

Patch diff:
--- a/src/fulfillment/events.py
+++ b/src/fulfillment/events.py
@@ -24,6 +24,9 @@
     if event_type != "charge.succeeded":
         return "ignored"
 
+    if event_id in processed_events:
+        return "duplicate_ignored"
+
     payload = event["data"]
     sku = str(payload["sku"])
     quantity = int(payload["quantity"])

content.notes: To rollback, remove the 3-line idempotency guard block added after the event_type check in src/fulfillment/events.py, or revert the file using git.

results.title: How to Implement Webhook Idempotency
results.snippet: Automatic retries are the primary source of duplicate webhook deliveries. When a delivery attempt fails — due to a timeout, server error, or network issue — the provider (or your webhook infrastructure) retries the same event.
snippets: Automatic retries are the primary source of duplicate webhook deliveries. When a delivery attempt fails — due to a timeout, server error, or network issue — the provider (or your webhook infrastructure) retries the same event.

Automatic retries are the primary source of duplicate webhook deliveries. When a delivery attempt fails — due to a timeout, server error, or network issue — the provider (or your webhook infrastructure) retries the same event. Without idempotent handlers, every retry triggers your business logic again.

Otherwise, late retries will slip through. Event replay — deliberately re-delivering events for debugging or recovery — is one of the most powerful tools in webhook infrastructure. After an outage, a bug fix, or a configuration change, you can replay all failed events to recover without data loss. Replay is only safe when your handlers are idempotent. Without idempotency, replaying events that were partially processed could cause duplicate side effects.

It automatically suppresses duplicate deliveries before they reach your handlers using content-based deduplication, groups related failures into Issues so you can see root causes and bulk-retry affected events with one click, and retries failed deliveries with configurable backoff so transient errors never produce duplicate side effects. Every delivery is traced end-to-end with full payload, headers, and response data. Get started with Hookdeck to add production-grade reliability to your webhook pipeline without writing the queueing code yourself. When a product manager asks for "guaranteed delivery," the correct answer is that delivery cannot be guaranteed exactly once — but processing can be made exactly-once through idempotency plus reconciliation. Frame the requirement as a processing guarantee and the engineering becomes tractable. Frame it as a delivery guarantee and you are chasing a theorem that says no. Treating delivery as exactly-once is a category error; build for duplicates by default. ... Idempotency is the consumer's job, keyed on a stable event ID.Every well-designed webhook carries a unique event ID that stays constant across retries — Stripe's event id, Shopify's X-Shopify-Webhook-Id, Svix's webhook-id.

The implication is that duplicate handling is your responsibility, not the provider's. You make processing idempotent so that receiving an event a second time produces no second side effect, rather than relying on the network to never duplicate. Why is exactly-once delivery impossible, and what can I do instead? How do I implement idempotency in a webhook consumer? What's the difference between Stripe, Shopify, and Svix retry behavior?

The practical consequence is a clean distinction worth carrying into every design review: exactly-once is achievable as a processing guarantee, never as a delivery guarantee. The wire will sometimes deliver a duplicate. What you control is whether processing that duplicate produces a second side effect. That is the entire job of idempotency, and it sits on the consumer side — the receiver — not the sender.

If your handler is not idempotent, that results in duplicate orders, duplicate charges, or duplicate emails. This guide explains how retries work and how to build handlers that are safe to call multiple times. ... A webhook delivery is an HTTP request from someone else's server to yours.

The sender sees a timeout, marks delivery as failed, and retries — even though your handler may have completed the work. This is the most common source of duplicate processing. An operation is idempotent if applying it multiple times produces the same result as applying it once. In the context of webhook handlers, this means: processing the same event twice must not create duplicate side effects.

Deduplication at the handler entry point handles exact retries (same delivery ID). You also need to design the operations inside your handler to be safe when run multiple times — because not all duplicate deliveries carry the same delivery ID. When creating or updating records, use upsert semantics — insert if not exists, update if exists.

Your dedup logic must also handle business-level deduplication — checking whether the underlying entity (payment, order) has already been processed, not just the delivery. Enqueue the event and do external calls in a background worker. If your handler creates an order but crashes before sending the confirmation email, the retry will attempt both steps again. Design each step to be independently idempotent, not just the handler entry point.

observation.content:
"""Webhook event processing for fulfillment."""

from __future__ import annotations

from typing import Any

from .inventory import InventoryLedger
from .notifications import NotificationSink


def process_webhook_event(
    event: dict[str, Any],
    *,
    ledger: InventoryLedger,
    notifier: NotificationSink,
    processed_events: set[str],
) -> str:
    """Process a payment-provider webhook event.

    BUG: duplicate event IDs are not checked before side effects, so provider
    redelivery can reserve inventory and notify fulfillment more than once.
    """

    event_id = str(event["event_id"])
    event_type = str(event["type"])
    if event_type != "charge.succeeded":
        return "ignored"

    payload = event["data"]
    sku = str(payload["sku"])
    quantity = int(payload["quantity"])
    order_id = str(payload["order_id"])

    ledger.reserve(sku=sku, quantity=quantity, event_id=event_id)
    notifier.send_reserved(order_id=order_id, event_id=event_id, sku=sku, quantity=quantity)
    processed_events.add(event_id)
    return "reserved"

content.notes: Added an idempotency guard `if event_id in processed_events: return 'duplicate_ignored'` immediately after the `event_type` check and before any side effects. This prevents duplicate inventory reservations and notifications for repeated successful event deliveries, while allowing retries for events that failed before being added to `processed_events`.

Insertion point: After `if event_type != 'charge.succeeded': return 'ignored'`
Guard logic: `if event_id in processed_events: return 'duplicate_ignored'`

--- a/src/fulfillment/events.py
+++ b/src/fulfillment/events.py
@@ -24,6 +24,9 @@

         return "ignored"
 
+    if event_id in processed_events:
+        return "duplicate_ignored"
+
     payload = event["data"]
     sku = str(payload["sku"])
     quantity = int(payload["quantity"])

final_files.README.md:
# Fulfillment Webhook Service

This mock service receives payment-provider webhook events after checkout. A `charge.succeeded`
event reserves inventory and sends a fulfillment notification. Providers may redeliver the same
event, so `event_id` must be idempotent: the same event can be retried safely, but it must not
reserve inventory or notify fulfillment twice.

Important behavior:

- Duplicate delivery of the same successful event must return `duplicate_ignored`.
- A new event with a different `event_id` may reserve inventory normally.
- If an event fails before side effects complete, it should not be marked processed.
- Keep fixes small and prefer changing `src/fulfillment/events.py` only unless evidence proves otherwise.

"""Webhook event processing for fulfillment."""

from __future__ import annotations

from typing import Any

from .inventory import InventoryLedger
from .notifications import NotificationSink


def process_webhook_event(
    event: dict[str, Any],
    *,
    ledger: InventoryLedger,
    notifier: NotificationSink,
    processed_events: set[str],
) -> str:
    """Process a payment-provider webhook event.

    BUG: duplicate event IDs are not checked before side effects, so provider
    redelivery can reserve inventory and notify fulfillment more than once.
    """

    event_id = str(event["event_id"])
    event_type = str(event["type"])
    if event_type != "charge.succeeded":
        return "ignored"

    payload = event["data"]
    sku = str(payload["sku"])
    quantity = int(payload["quantity"])
    order_id = str(payload["order_id"])

    ledger.reserve(sku=sku, quantity=quantity, event_id=event_id)
    notifier.send_reserved(order_id=order_id, event_id=event_id, sku=sku, quantity=quantity)
    processed_events.add(event_id)
    return "reserved"
final_files.tests/test_webhook_idempotency.py:
from src.fulfillment.events import process_webhook_event
from src.fulfillment.inventory import InventoryLedger
from src.fulfillment.notifications import NotificationSink


def _event(event_id: str, *, sku: str = "sku-chair", quantity: int = 2) -> dict:
    return {
        "event_id": event_id,
        "type": "charge.succeeded",
        "data": {
            "order_id": "order-1001",
            "sku": sku,
            "quantity": quantity,
        },
    }


def test_duplicate_webhook_delivery_is_idempotent() -> None:
    ledger = InventoryLedger({"sku-chair": 5})
    notifier = NotificationSink()
    processed_events: set[str] = set()

    first = process_webhook_event(_event("evt-1"), ledger=ledger, notifier=notifier, processed_events=processed_events)
    duplicate = process_webhook_event(_event("evt-1"), ledger=ledger, notifier=notifier, processed_events=processed_events)

    assert first == "reserved"
    assert duplicate == "duplicate_ignored"
    assert ledger.stock["sku-chair"] == 3
    assert len(ledger.audit_log) == 1
    assert len(notifier.messages) == 1
    assert processed_events == {"evt-1"}


def test_distinct_events_still_reserve_inventory() -> None:
    ledger = InventoryLedger({"sku-chair": 5})
    notifier = NotificationSink()
    processed_events: set[str] = set()

    process_webhook_event(_event("evt-1"), ledger=ledger, notifier=notifier, processed_events=processed_events)
    process_webhook_event(_event("evt-2"), ledger=ledger, notifier=notifier, processed_events=processed_events)

    assert ledger.stock["sku-chair"] == 1
    assert len(ledger.audit_log) == 2
    assert len(notifier.messages) == 2
    assert processed_events == {"evt-1", "evt-2"}


def test_failed_event_is_not_marked_processed_before_retry() -> None:
    ledger = InventoryLedger({"sku-chair": 5})
    notifier = NotificationSink()
    processed_events: set[str] = set()

    try:
        process_webhook_event(_event("evt-missing", sku="sku-missing"), ledger=ledger, notifier=notifier, processed_events=processed_events)
    except KeyError:
        pass

    assert processed_events == set()
    assert notifier.messages == []
    assert ledger.stock["sku-chair"] == 5
